results.possibleIdentity: Robert (Bob) Fiala was an experimental filmmaker and artistic director active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization promoting collaborations between artists and engineers. In 1968, Fiala collaborated on a project for "Intermedia '68" at the Brooklyn Academy of Music, working with Carolee Schneemann's kinetic dance group. His work focused on the development of cinemagraphics and experimental film animation, often utilizing Bolex Super 8 cameras, macrophotography, and polarized light. He was also the Artistic Director of the Multigravitational Group (also known as the Multigravitational Aerodance Group), an experimental performance ensemble based in New York that utilized scaffolding for aerial dance. In the late 1960s, he received bursaries from the Canada Council for his work in cinema and photography while based in Montreal.

results.possibleIdentity: Bob Ebers (born 1926) is a New York-based sculptor and artist active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for creating 'Funnyphones,' a series of whimsical sculptures constructed from repurposed telephone components and mixed media such as plywood and lucite. His work aimed to humanize technology and was described in contemporary publications like Playboy (1972) as starting a 'bell epoch in art.' Ebers frequently utilized telephone receivers, dials, and internal mechanisms to create figures with human-like features, such as the 'Mad Professor' or 'Uncle Sam.' Notable works include 'Ebers Phone (1972),' 'The Brain,' 'Alexander Graham Crump,' and 'The Lucite Gig Dial.' His artistic focus on the intersection of technology and art align with the era's collaborative movements, such as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to bridge the gap between engineers and artists.

results.possibleIdentity: William "Bill" King (1925–2015) was a prominent American contemporary sculptor known for his lanky, elongated, and often humorous figurative works. Born in Jacksonville, Florida, he initially studied engineering at the University of Florida before moving to New York in 1945 to study architecture and sculpture at the Cooper Union, graduating in 1948. His career spanned several decades, and he was particularly active in the late 1960s and 1970s, during which time his work offered a figurative alternative to the then-dominant movements of Minimalism and Conceptual Art. King worked with a diverse array of materials, including aluminum, vinyl, wood, bronze, and burlap. He held several high-profile positions in the art world, serving as the president of the National Academy of Design from 1994 to 1998 and being elected to the American Academy of Arts and Letters. While primarily known for his satirical and social realist sculptures, his early background in engineering and his presence in the New York art scene during the 1960s align him with the period of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, and his work has been included in major exhibitions alongside E.A.T. founders and associates like Robert Rauschenberg.

results.possibleIdentity: James F. Umland (1928–2016) was an American conceptual artist and educator active in the New York City art scene during the 1960s and 1970s. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and his work was included in the 1968–1969 E.A.T. competition exhibition titled 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Umland is recognized as a contributor to the emerging conceptual art movement; his name appears in Lucy Lippard's seminal chronicle 'Six Years: The Dematerialization of the Art Object from 1966 to 1972.' In 1970, artist Robert Barry recommended Umland for inclusion in the 'Art in the Mind' exhibition at Oberlin College. His career also featured exhibitions at the Westerly Gallery and the Brooklyn Museum of Art. Aside from his studio practice, Umland hosted a radio program on WBAI-FM in New York, where he interviewed creative figures such as John Cage, Jacques D'Amboise, and Dave Brubeck. He earned a BFA from the University of Iowa and a Master's degree from New York University, later teaching art in high schools and colleges in the New York area until his retirement.
